prompts.prompt: I need to change the shipping cost calculation inside Invoice.getValue(). The result flows up through BillingStatement.makeStatement(). Should I write my tests against getValue or makeStatement?
expected_concepts: getValue is closer to the change point — fewer logical inference steps
makeStatement is a valid interception point but each extra hop makes it harder to know the test is actually catching the right thing
prefer closest accessible public method on the class being changed
makeStatement is appropriate only if getValue itself is hard to reach from tests

prompts.prompt: I have to make changes in three related classes — OrderProcessor, TaxCalculator, and ShippingEstimator — and none of them have tests. Do I really need to break dependencies on all three separately before I can test?
expected_concepts: draw an effect sketch from all three change points
look for a pinch point — a single place where all three effect chains converge
if a pinch point exists, one test suite covers all three changes
only need to break dependencies to reach the pinch point, not each class individually

prompts.prompt: I changed a private helper method deep inside a reporting class. I have a test that calls the top-level generateReport() method and it still passes. Can I trust that the test is actually covering my change?
expected_concepts: each step between change point and interception point is a logical inference
more steps means more ways the test can pass for the wrong reason
verify sensitivity: make a small deliberate break at the change point and confirm the test fails
closer interception point (nearer the private method, via a public method that calls it) would be more reliable

prompts.prompt: I'm tracing effects from a change point in UserProfile.updatePreferences() and I can see it affects NotificationService.send(), AuditLog.record(), and RecommendationEngine.recompute(). Is there a systematic way to decide how many test locations I need?
expected_concepts: draw the effect sketch from the change point across all three downstream methods
look for a node reachable from all three — that is the pinch point
if all three share a common caller or pass through a shared method, test there
if no convergence exists, test at each of the three methods as close to the change as possible

prompts.prompt: How do I write a characterization test for a method that reads from a database?
expected_concepts: this is about characterization test technique and dependency breaking, not interception point selection
welc-characterization-test or welc-seam-model are more relevant

prompts.prompt: I need to add new audit logging to a legacy class but I can't test it. Should I use Sprout Method or Wrap Method?
expected_concepts: this is a sprout vs wrap decision for adding new behavior to untested code
welc-sprout-wrap-decision is the relevant skill
interception point selection is not the question here — the question is how to add behavior safely

prompts.prompt: My class has a tight coupling to a third-party HTTP client and I can't instantiate it in a test harness. How do I break this dependency?
expected_concepts: this is a separation problem — the class cannot be instantiated
welc-seam-model and welc-sensing-vs-separation are relevant
interception point selection presupposes the class can already be reached from test code

prompts.prompt: I found what I think is a pinch point at BillingStatement.makeStatement that covers all my change points. But makeStatement has complex dependencies of its own. Should I still use it as my interception point?
expected_concepts: a pinch point is only useful if it is accessible — complex dependencies may mean significant dependency-breaking work just to reach it
evaluate whether breaking dependencies at the pinch point is less effort than testing each change point individually
if pinch point is too costly, fall back to testing each change point at its nearest accessible interception point
seam work (welc-seam-model) may be needed before the pinch point can be used

prompts.prompt: My effect sketch shows that a change in one method affects twenty different downstream methods. I can't find a single pinch point. What do I do?
expected_concepts: when the effect sketch fans out widely with no convergence, the change set is probably too large
narrow scope: find pinch points for subsets of one or two changes at a time
if still no pinch point, test individual changes as close to each change point as possible
a wide tangled effect sketch is also a design signal — the code likely has poor encapsulation

prompts.prompt: I want to use integration tests against our REST API to cover a set of changes I'm making in the domain layer. Is that a valid interception point strategy?
expected_concepts: the REST endpoint is a valid interception point — all effects will eventually reach the API response
but it is a very distant interception point: each logical step between domain change and HTTP response is an inference that can fail silently
test failures at this distance are hard to diagnose and the test may pass for unrelated reasons
better approach: use the API test as a coarse pinch point to start, then add closer tests at the class collaboration boundary once dependencies are broken
